data.title: Fortive: Slimmer And More Focused, But Not Clearly Better
data.description:
Fortive Corporation (FTV) has been casting around for the right model for quite a few years now, and as I’ve noted in prior articles, it has at times felt less like management was following a real plan or philosophy and more like whatever was in vogue with the Street at the time – aggressive growth by M&A, an intense focus on recurring revenue and SaaS business models, electrification/AI, and now business model simplification through spinning off Vontier (VNT) and Ralliant (RAL).

I’m not altogether surprised, then, that Fortive hasn’t done much since my last article, with the shares barely up on an adjusted basis and trailing the broader industrial space by close to 20%. Fortive’s odd mix of businesses makes it hard to benchmark against specific companies, but I’ll note that a grab-bag of companies that compete with Fortive businesses – including Keysight (KEYS), Mirion (MIR), MSA Safety (MSA), Steris (STE), and Trimble (TRMB) – have all outperformed Fortive to varying degrees.

Given that Fortive isn’t particularly short-cycle sensitive, I don’t think an improving industrial outlook is all that much of a catalyst for them. Moreover, I need to see more evidence that management can use strategic reinvestment into the business to drive better than 4% revenue growth and mid-20%’s FCF margins, as those metrics aren’t enough to drive a compelling fair value for me.

An Uptick In Guidance And Growth Would Be Welcome

Fortive did manage to beat expectations in the third quarter, but it didn’t leave me all that convinced that there would be strong follow through into the fourth quarter. Revenue was 2% better than expected (with both Intelligent Operating Solutions, or IOS, and Advanced Healthcare Solutions, or AHS, beating), but organic growth was under 2% and not particularly impressive in a quarter when several industrials saw an uptick in organic growth.

The IOS business should pick up some on continued strength in professional instrumentation and gas detection, and I would expect to see Fluke start to improve as well. Facility and Asset Lifecycle is a tougher call with ongoing weakness in non-residential construction (ABI below 50) and declines in non-resi starts, as well as weaker government spending.

I don’t see much reason to expect a sudden surge in the AHS business. While management has talked about cautious spending among healthcare providers, I’m not seeing that in the results from companies like Steris, Getinge (GNGBY), or Mirion, and I don’t really see what would drive a sudden reversal/catchup.

With the Street looking for around 3% revenue growth next year (at the low end of the 3%-5% growth target range provided at the last investor day), it may take guidance for organic growth of 3.5% or better to get the Street a little more enthusiastic about the near-term, as Fortive isn’t particularly well-positioned for a short-cycle recovery given its business mix and end-market market exposures.

I’d also like to see some organic, repeatable upside to margins in the fourth quarter and in FY’26 guidance. Adjusted operating profits did beat expectations by about 12% last quarter, but higher capitalization in the software businesses and adjustments to incentive compensation helped drive about 20% of that beat and aren’t expect to recur in Q4. Still, credit where due – opex was meaningfully lower than expected even without those benefits and that does offer some credible grounds to look for upgraded guidance when Fortive next reports.

The New Fortive Is… Fine - But Where’s The Sizzle?

There’s nothing wrong with a high-quality boring business that churns out solid free cash flow year in and year out. As is, I think Fortive is well-positioned to do that.

The past fixation on recurring revenue may have been overdone, but the reality is that it left Fortive with a business mix that generates solid, high-margin revenue on a relatively consistent basis, and not many industrials could hope to consistently generate 20%-plus free cash flow margins. That, in turn, means ample cash flow that can be reinvested back into the business (R&D and new product development), used on complementary acquisitions, or returned to shareholders through buybacks and dividends.

Even so, the reality is that Fortive has meaningfully derated over the years. Back when M&A-driven “compounders” like Ametek (AME), Danaher (DHR), Nordson (NDSN), Roper (ROP), and Fortive were in vogue, it wasn’t uncommon for them to trade at 20x or more times forward EBITDA. Now Fortive trades at around 17x my ’26 EBITDA estimate and core profitability metrics haven’t really deteriorated meaningfully.

I think part of the issue is that there’s not as much to get excited about with this business. Fluke’s electronic test and measurement tools are critical in maintenance and condition monitoring, and leveraged to ongoing growth in industrial capex, but there isn’t really a solid “above market” growth driver.

The Facility and Asset Lifecycle businesses likewise aren’t really leveraged to growth drivers apart from an eventual turnaround and recovery in non-residential building activity. Moreover, while I could see share growth and maybe increased adoption driving some upside, there’s also a risk that government spending won’t recover to past levels.

Other businesses like gas detection are fine, but relatively small. Turning to healthcare, I’ve written before that I don’t especially love this collection of assets. Landauer is a solid, stable business and I feel the same about Provation, but I have had my concerns about the competitiveness of ASP given a lack of product innovation, and management’s efforts to target faster-growing market segments doesn’t appear to have yielded much.

To reiterate, there’s nothing wrong with a business (or collection of businesses) that can grow fairly reliably around 3%-5% with less cyclicality and well above-average cash flow generation. The problem is how the market prices these stories; right now the market is infatuated with data center leverage, as well (to varying degrees) as themes like automation, electrification, aerospace, and asset intelligence.

Other than some plays on sterilization, inspection, and asset intelligence, Fortive really isn’t well-placed in those megatrends. That’ll likely be a positive when they inevitably cool (as past fads like IIOT, smart cities, and M&A-driven recurring/software revenue did), but in the meantime it could make rerating more challenging.

The Outlook

I don’t have any major issues with management’s investor day guide for 3% to 5% growth through 2027, and I’m modeling around 4% growth over the next four to five years and a little over 4% over the long term, as I expect solid ongoing demand across the company’s major end-markets (government spending the main exception).

I’m a little more hesitant to buy into the 50-100bp/year EBITDA expansion target, particularly if management is serious about wanting to reinvest in R&D to drive improved depth and breadth of new product introductions and product vitality. The subscription nature of the software businesses will certainly help, as should a focus on increased recurring revenue from Fluke and possibly some improvements within the healthcare businesses, but I expecting less EBITDA margin accretion over the next three to five years (around 100-150bp).

I do see Fortive becoming more cash-efficient (software businesses help), and I’m expecting free cash flow margin improvement above and beyond EBITDA margin expansion toward and past the mid-20%’s over the next decade, driving adjusted FCF growth of around 6% to 7%.

Neither discounted cash flow or margin/return-driven EV/EBITDA produce an exciting fair value today. The former suggests a mid-single-digit long-term annualized total potential return, while a 16.25x forward EBITDA multiple (with a 200bp premium embedded to account for quality advantages like the recurring/software revenue contributions) doesn’t get me past the low-$50’s.

The Bottom Line

If Fortive management can guide for stronger organic revenue growth and better margins in 2026, I can see a path to faster rerating. As is, I think this is a business that needs to prove to the Street that it does, in fact, have a core strategy and will stick with it for more than a couple of years. That may sound harsh, but with Fortive underperforming the broader industrial space by a significant margin over the past five years, the onus is on management to reinvigorate sentiment.

data.title: QQQI: 0.02% SEC Yield Vs The 14% Distribution Yield
data.description:
We recently penned a piece on why the covered call funds explosion was just another symptom of the bubble in place. We also described why such funds are likely to be massive losers over the next bear cycle. We did not think we would get a chance to write on a different kind of covered call fund so quickly after that. Today we cover NEOS Nasdaq-100® High Income ETF (QQQI) and go over why this fund is different than the run-of-the-mill buy write funds.

The Strategy

The fund summarizes its strategy with the following.

The Fund seeks to distribute high monthly income generated from investing in the constituents of the Nasdaq-100 Index and implementing a data-driven call option strategy

Actively managed by NEOS, the Fund seeks to take advantage of tax loss harvesting opportunities in addition to utilizing NDX Index options classified as section 1256 contracts, which are subject to lower 60/40 tax rates

The Fund utilizes a call option strategy that may include both sold and purchased NDX index options, which may provide the opportunity for upside capture in rising equity markets

Source: QQQI

The key take home messages are that the fund is actively managed, uses tax advantage of futures contracts and may use call spreads to prevent trailing in parabolic markets. We have nothing against using the futures to sell calls on equity exposure. Futures actually trade longer hours and are generally 99.99% correlated to underlying equities. You do have some time value and interest rate changes which can create brief deviations, but on the whole, this works. We also like the active management idea, as the buy-write funds have just not gotten the message and that blindly selling calls is a recipe for long term problems.

Performance

The fund has done really well against the CBOE Nasdaq-100 BuyWrite Monthly Index. Note the amounts since inception are cumulative (not annualized). Also note that the fund is relatively recent, starting on January 30, 2024.

So right off the bat, we have to appreciate the outperformance relative to the index. How is the fund tracking so much higher? The answer is actually in the same graphic above. It is selling less option premium relative to its benchmark and hence tracking closer to the Nasdaq 100 index. How do you sell less option premium compared to the benchmark? There are a few ways you can do it. One, you can just sell a further out of the money option than what the index dictates.

The Cboe Nasdaq 100 BuyWrite IndexSM (BXN) applies the buywrite strategy to the Nasdaq 100® Index (NDX). The objective of a buwrite strategy is to buffer downside returns by selling a slice of upside returns. The BXN tracks the value of hypothetical portfolio that overlays a short at-the-money call option on the NDX (NDX call). The premium harvested from the NDX call buffers possible losses on an investment in Nasdaq 100 stocks. The BXN Index portfolio is calculated every 15 seconds and is rebalanced monthly after the expiration of the NDX call, typically 11 am ET every third Friday.

Source: CBOE

The fund appears to be doing this. With the Nasdaq 100 at 25,600, the calls written currently are at 26,000 and 26,400.

What calls would you see on the CBOE Nasdaq Buy-Write index? Well, the Nasdaq 100 was around 25,346 at the end of the last monthly option expiration cycle.

So the calls sold would likely be close to 25,400. QQQI is selling calls far above that level and hence making its performance closer to that of the regular Nasdaq 100 index.

There is also another way that you can sell less option premium relative to the index and that is also apparent here. The fund is selling calls on less than 100% of its holdings. So all of that has helped performance as we are in a bull market (in case you didn't know).

Yield

This part is still fairly funny to us as the fund is paying a very large yield and this of course gets everyone out of the woodwork to start chasing. But the math is right there on the front page.

If you want our 2 cents on the actual yield, you can just go with the SEC yield, which funnily enough is actually 2 cents on every $100 dollars. The yield math is easy to figure out. The Nasdaq 100 has basically no underlying yield. That is the product of horrendous valuations on growth stocks. The Invesco QQQ Trust ETF (QQQ) yields 0.45%.

QQQI has a much higher expense ratio so that should push the SEC yield negative. But there is some option income there, so maybe that pushes it back into positive territory? Who knows. But that 14% distribution rate is coming from capital appreciation. So if you think that is sustainable, as in you can get it and the NAV can stay stable or increase, you have to think this madness is sustainable.

Good luck with that.

Verdict

We think QQQ likely falls 50%-80% from here. So obviously a fund that rides on that index, won't make our long side cut. The "less" option selling has worked in the bull phase and likely backfires spectacularly in a bear market. Nonetheless, maybe the managers turn out to be good traders and dodge some of the downfall in the market? We wouldn't want to be betting on that outcome at the end of the bubble. We like generating income via covered calls and it is not hard to get 14% to 23% annualized returns on quality companies if you wait for the right price. You also have to use longer dated options to have any chance of this actually working. But we wouldn't have a chance of doing that on an index using shorter dated options, let alone the worst priced index of all time. We rate the fund a Strong Sell.

Please note that this is not financial advice. It may seem like it, sound like it, but surprisingly, it is not. Investors are expected to do their own due diligence and consult a professional who knows their objectives and constraints. Most of our articles are not written for "buy and hold" investors. Our articles are not written for investors who ignore capital preservation while focusing only on yield.

data.title: Veeva Systems: Veeva AI Comes At A Steep Cost (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Introduction & Investment Thesis

At the start of 2025, I had selected Veeva Systems Inc. (VEEV) to be my software pick for 2025. Up until the first 6 months, Veeva was indeed outperforming the software space, with the stock up 38% during this period of time.

Unfortunately, after staying fairly resilient until the start of November, the stock lost over 25% after its Q3 FY26 earnings, where management pointed to a slowdown in revenue growth and margin contraction in Q4.

Not only that, despite the management reiterating its $6B revenue target by 2030, investors became concerned about the competitive landscape with Salesforce (CRM) after fewer than expected Top 20 biopharma companies selected Vault CRM.

Finally, what is deeply concerning to me is that the path to $6B in revenue comes at a substantial cost of 1000 basis points of margin contraction.

Despite Veeva’s competitive advantage of building deep industry-specific applications, I am forced to downgrade VEEV stock to a Hold at the moment, with a significantly lower price target than before at $236 per share, representing minimal upside from current levels.

On Track For $6B In Revenue By 2030, Veeva AI To Provide Another Tailwind

Veeva reported its Q3 FY26 earnings in November, where revenue slightly declined sequentially, growing 16% YoY, but still came above estimates, while the non-GAAP operating margin expanded to 45%.

In October, when Veeva held their Investor Day, they reiterated their $6B revenue target by 2030. In terms of revenue breakdown by segments, the management broke it down as follows, where they notably expect the revenue contribution from their Clinical segment (that houses Veeva Development Cloud) to grow from 21% to 30%, while the contribution from Commercial (that houses Vault CRM) to shrink from 45% to 35%.

While the management emphasized that Clinical is their largest area of opportunity in life sciences, Veeva continued to make substantial progress in their Commercial Cloud, where they added 23 new Vault CRM customers during the quarter, along with 115 Vault CRM customers that are now live, demonstrating the expertise of their services to support the complexity of global biopharma.

In all of this, the center of attention was on Veeva AI, where management announced that the first Veeva AI agents will be available in December for CRM and commercial content, with plans to roll out R&D, quality, and additional commercial agents in 2026. Here’s a brief timeline of the rollout plan for Veeva AI that management shared during their Investor Day presentation.

Based on management commentary, they have already started working with some of their early adopters, where results are “very promising.” As I wrote in my previous post, the monetization from Veeva AI will be twofold, where the first layer of monetization will be the platform and the second layer of monetization will be from industry-specific agents that customers will get access to when they purchase Veeva AI.

3 Reasons Why Veeva Fell Out Of Favor After Q3

However, Veeva stock significantly dropped after its Q3 earnings for three main reasons, which, I believe, are substantial.

First, when it comes to forward guidance, management guided for Q4 revenue growth to slow down by 400 basis points sequentially to just 12% YoY growth, with non-GAAP operating margin contracting 200 basis points to 43%.

Second, when it comes to Vault CRM migration, management now expects 14 of the top 20 biopharmas to select Vault CRM, slightly fewer than they originally expected to win. When analysts probed further on potential revenue risks from migration hiccups, management responded that CRM is now a smaller part of revenue and no impact is expected this year or next. Having said that, the competitive landscape with Salesforce can’t be ignored, especially after they said that they were “killing Veeva” in the Life Sciences Cloud, which I discussed in my previous post.

Third, while I argued that Veeva’s competitive strength lies in developing deep industry-specific applications, its path to achieving $6B in revenue by FY30 comes at a significant cost of contracted margins. Compared to the 45% non-GAAP operating margins at current levels, management expects that it will drop to 35%+ as they pursue Veeva AI and expand into new markets to reach their $6B goal.

Revisiting My Valuation

Taking the management’s revenue projections of $6B by 2030 and non-GAAP operating margin of 35% into account, we arrive at a non-GAAP operating income of $2.11B by FY31, which will be equivalent to a present value of $1.38B when discounted at 8%.

The stock is currently trading at a non-GAAP forward P/E ratio of 28. Assuming the same P/E ratio for its FY31 earnings, we arrive at a price target of $236 per share, which represents an upside of just 6% from current levels.

The way I see it, either Veeva has to revise their 2030 revenue target much higher or offer a path to achieving their $6B target without sacrificing 1000 basis points of profit margins.

My Final Verdict And Conclusions

In my earlier post, I argued why Veeva is well positioned to compete against Salesforce given its competitive advantage in building deep industry-level applications. I had also expressed my enthusiasm for the opportunity with Veeva AI.

However, Veeva’s Q3 was quite alarming when it said that it expects fewer Top 20 biopharmas to select Vault CRM, while projecting revenue to slow down and margins to contract in the next quarter.

Most importantly, up until now, my valuation on Veeva has always been based on the assumption that the company will reach its $6B revenue target by FY31 with little damage to the bottom line. However, a 1000 basis point margin contraction is too big a price to pay in my opinion.

With the revised margin projection, Veeva’s fair value is much lower than my previous estimate at $236 per share. As a result, I will downgrade the stock to a Hold at the moment, with limited upside potential at current levels.

data.title: VRP: Preferred ETF With Outstanding Risk-Adjusted Return
data.description:
This article updates my review from March in light of current holdings and recent performance.

VRP strategy

Invesco Variable Rate Preferred ETF (VRP) was launched on May 1, 2014 and tracks the ICE Variable Rate Preferred & Hybrid Securities Index. VRP has 330 holdings, a 30-day SEC yield of 5.30%, a trailing 12-month yield of 6.53%, a yield-to-worst of 5.79% and a total expense ratio of 0.50%. Distributions are paid monthly. As described in the prospectus by Invesco, the underlying index…

…is a market capitalization-weighted index designed to track the performance of floating and variable rate investment grade and below investment grade U.S. dollar denominated preferred stock, as well as certain types of hybrid securities

Eligible securities must have at least one day remaining to maturity, at least 18 months to maturity at the time of issuance, and meet other requirements. The portfolio turnover rate was 20% in the most recent fiscal year and 16% in the previous year.

Preferred stocks have priority over common stocks in the payment of dividends and proceeds of liquidation, but are generally junior to debt and do not carry voting rights. They are considered hybrid assets and their price behavior is between stocks and high-yield bonds. Variable or floating rate preferred stocks pay dividends at rates that adjust periodically to reflect market interest rates.

Portfolio

VRP is mostly invested in securities issued by U.S.-domiciled companies (86.5% of asset value), with a focus on financials (62.7%). Utilities and energy weigh 17.2% and 12.6%, while other sectors are below 5%.

Securities with an investment grade rating represent 82% of assets (BBB and better per S&P or equivalent), pointing to moderate credit risk in aggregate.

The interest rate risk is also moderate, with an effective duration of 2.81 years.

The portfolio is quite concentrated, and risks related to some companies are significant. Indeed, the top 10 issuers, listed in the table below, represent 38.9% of asset value, and the heaviest one (Citigroup) is close to 8%. Five major banks are in the top 6.

Invesco Variable Rate Preferred ETF (VRP) is a preferred stocks ETF combining relative low risks in several dimensions: credit risk, interest rate risk, capital decay and price volatility. data.title: BioMarin Pharmaceuticals: Amicus Transforms Business Case Overnight
data.description:
By the end of October, I concluded that BioMarin Pharmaceutical Inc. (BMRN) was showing growing numbers, yet it came with falling expectations. Shares had fallen to the lowest levels in a decade, despite profitable growth and a strong net cash position, even as some growth momentum was slowing down.

This investment case transformed overnight after the company announced a giant deal to acquire Amicus towards year end, set to leverage up the business significantly, with relatively few tangible near term benefits seen.

The positive share price reaction surprises me somewhat, also standing at the basis of my cautious take here.

Some Perspective

Other than a brief momentum run in 2015, which took many biopharmaceutical firms along for the ride, shares of BioMarin peaked around the $100 mark in 2020.

This changed after the company received a Complete Response Letter from the FDA for valoctogene raxoparvovec gene therapy for the treatment of severe hemophilia A, causing shares to crater to $75 overnight.

This share price and resulting valuation were applied to a business that generated $1.70 billion in sales in 2019, on which adjusted earnings of $167 million were reported, with minimal GAAP losses of $24 million reported. As such, BioMarin was posting realistic break-even levels, yet it should be noted that some $700 million was spent on R&D efforts, equal to 40% of sales!

For the past five years, BioMarin has grown and diversified to generate over $2.8 billion in sales, with revenues having grown by about two-thirds in just five years time. Adjusted earnings were reported at $3.52 per share, but this included about a dollar in stock-based compensation expenses, with earnings otherwise seen around $2.50 per share. Moreover, the company guided for 2025 sales to advance to a midpoint of $3.15 billion, seeing adjusted earnings around $4.30 per share.

A net cash position of over a billion dollars would come down a bit following a quarter of a billion deal for Inozyme Pharma, as announced in the spring.

So far, so good, as the business saw growth decelerate to 4% in the third quarter, driven by softer growth in Voxzogo, its enzyme group, as well as the business exploring strategic alternatives for a potential growth darling, Roctavian.

Valuation Perspectives

Following the release of the third-quarter results, the company guided for full-year sales of around $3.175 billion, with adjusted earnings seen around $3.55 per share, down quite a bit from the previous outlook, driven by IPR&D charges related to the Inozyme acquisition. With 192 million shares trading around the $52 mark, the equity valuation of the firm stood at $10 billion, this including a $1.4 billion net cash position. Operating asset valuations of $8.6 billion valued operations at less than 3 times sales, with real earnings support coming in.

However, with operating assets trading around $45 per share, earnings power around $3 and change looked very cheap at a low double-digit earnings multiple. This comes as investors were digesting more competition for top-selling drug Voxzogo, while Rocavian was acknowledged to be a failure now as well.

These created threats to a $4 billion revenue target for 2027, but weighing it all together, valuations have come in amidst continued doubts. Hence, I conclude that speculative appeal was apparent, always speculative if you are dealing with biotechnology names.

A Big Move

Over the past two months, shares of BioMarin have risen substantially from $52 to $60, as some big news has taken place recently. One piece of good news came days after the release of the third quarter results, as the company announced that the FDA accepted BioMarin's PALYNZIQ supplemental biologics license applications for priority review for adolescents aged between 12-17 years.

The bigger news came in the second half of December, when the business announced a huge $4.8 billion deal to acquire Amicus Therapeutics (FOLD), to establish a position in rare diseases. Under the terms of the deal, Amicus will be bought for $14.50 per share, marking a 33% premium over the unaffected closing price. This values equity at $4.8 billion, as the business operated with a net debt load just over a hundred million as well.

With the purchase, BioMarin will obtain products like Galafold, an oral treatment for Fabry disease, and Pombiliti + Opfolda, a two-component therapy for Pompe disease. Moreover, BioMarin will also obtain U.S. rights for DMX-200, a potential small molecule to treat focal segmental glomerulosclerosis.

These products generate $599 million in trailing sales, indicating that the purchase comes at a price of over 8 times sales, compared to just about 3 times for its own business. Net product sales already came in at $169 million in the third quarter, trending close to $675 million each year, while third-quarter sales were up 20% on the year before.

The deal has quite an impact. In between a pre-deal net cash position of around $1.4 billion, I see pro forma net debt of around $3.5 billion, as Amicus is essentially breaking even here. The company has not indicated the current level of leverage but hopes to deleverage to <2.5 times within two years, as the deal is considered accretive to 2027 earnings, with immediate gross leverage seen around 3.0-3.5 times.

Nonetheless, the deal is expensive, as a $4.9 billion deal (including modest net debt) is equivalent to about 57% of the prevailing pre-deal valuation of BioMarin, while the sales contribution of Amicus is seen just shy of 20%!

This huge valuation discrepancy is justified (apparently) by the potential and hopes for management that both products for Galafold and Pompe each can become a >$1 billion drug in the 2030s.

Moving Higher

Despite paying a big premium, higher sales multiples, and jacking up leverage, shares of BioMarin have moved convincingly higher in response to the deal announcement.

Shares rose nearly eight dollars on the announcement, adding about $1.5 billion to the market value of the firm in a single move. That move is big, with shares of acquiring firms typically falling. The move is furthermore substantial in relation to the value of the acquired assets. This comes after the deal ignites some growth, diversification, and also leaves the potential for synergies, with both firms targeting orphan diseases, creating potential synergies across marketing and distribution.

Frankly, I am surprised by the positive reaction, but the reality is that the investment thesis changed dramatically overnight. BioMarin is moving from a substantial net cash position to a substantial net debt load, yet adding some growth engines and diversification to the business as well.

This makes me a bit cautious here, also given the positive reaction. While I understand some state rationale for the moves seen, I am erring on the cautious side, certainly after valuations have increased quite a bit with the move higher, the premium paid, and the debt incurred.

data.title: Blowout Q3 GDP Turns The Market Narrative On Its Head
data.description:
Third quarter U.S. GDP numbers showed growth crushing consensus estimates. This serves as a bullish indicator for the stock market (SPY) because it shows that the economy may be on a much stronger footing than previously thought and that recent fiscal and trade policy changes may be having a more positive impact on the economy, according to government data, though economists remain divided on the durability of these effects.

As a result, the "economic slowdown is inevitable" narrative faces a fresh challenge from the latest economic data. In this article, I'm going to detail how I'm thinking about allocating capital as a result. Note that this analysis focuses on near-term economic and market implications rather than long-term policy outcomes or political considerations.

Q3 GDP Data Sends A Clear Signal

In Q3, the U.S.'s real GDP rose by a whopping 4.3%, which consisted of very strong consumer spending growth, plus a modest increase in exports, plus a very slight increase in government spending, all driving higher growth, while a modest decline in imports also led to higher GDP, since imports are subtracted from the net GDP calculation.

Rising exports and declining imports coincided with recent changes in U.S. trade policy that included higher tariffs and revised trade agreements that were designed to increase U.S. exports and reduce imports from trading partners. This is because higher tariffs make imports less competitive with domestically produced alternatives, and the revised trade agreements included increased market access for U.S. exports to foreign markets as well as, in some cases, pledges to increase purchases of U.S. products (such as weapons, soybeans, and energy, for example). Not only that, but underlying real demand, based on the BEA's measure of real final sales to private domestic purchasers, rose by 3%, which demonstrated that the strong economic growth was driven at least in part by strong underlying domestic demand.

Additionally, this growth was net of inflation, despite inflation numbers being elevated during the quarter, with the PCE price index, which is the Fed's preferred inflation metric, rising by 2.8% in the third quarter, and the core PCE index, which excludes the more volatile food and energy prices, rising by 2.9%.

This is a high inflation reading, which does not bode well for falling long-term interest rates or additional rate cuts (which would be a boost to the market). At the same time, however, it instills more confidence in the strong economic growth number because it shows that nominal GDP growth was even more impressive at more than 7% during the quarter and not simply due to an artificially low inflation number

In addition to trade policy changes, which included the opening of more foreign markets and a reduction in trade barriers for U.S. businesses while increasing tariffs on imports into the United States, which combined to have a positive effect on GDP in Q3, there were other reasons for the strong growth that were tied to recent policy changes. For example, the provisions in recent tax and spending legislation, which include accelerated depreciation and other domestic investment incentives, are intended to increase production in the United States and thereby reduce demand for foreign imports by replacing them with domestically-produced products in strategic industries. This helps to drive stronger economic growth through a lower net cost of investing in domestic production rather than importing new products, while also increasing competitiveness for U.S. output and making long-lived asset spending more attractive in the United States.

What This Means for Rates and the Fed

Of course, this is simply one quarter of results, and just because the numbers look good does not mean that the economy is set for a new higher-for-longer growth rate. Moreover, this is certainly not a political statement or even a blanket endorsement of the policies that produced this result, as these are purely short term, and the long-term impacts of lower taxes and higher government spending, as well as high tariffs, on the U.S. economy are far more complex and are beyond the scope of this article.

That being said, what can be concluded is that this result does materially impact my outlook in the near term for where the economy and interest rates are likely headed. For starters, the higher economic growth yet persistently high inflation numbers mean that the Fed is less likely than previously thought to cut rates at an aggressive clip late next year as it tries to balance its dual mandate of fighting inflation and supporting the economy. With the economy growing at a strong clip, rate cut support is less needed, and with inflation being persistently high, rate cuts are increasingly unattractive on that end of the spectrum.

Why BDCs Suddenly Look Much More Attractive

Perhaps the biggest winner from this news is the business company space (BIZD), with names like Ares Capital (ARCC), Blackstone Secured Lending (BXSL), Capital Southwest (CSWC), and even Main Street Capital (MAIN), along with many lower-quality names like FS KKR Capital (FSK) and others under pressure recently due to growing concerns of a weakening economy and accelerated Federal Reserve rate cuts, which would combine to likely reduce net investment income per share. This is because BDCs invest primarily in floating-rate loans that are indexed to short-term interest rates. Additionally, their loans tend to be more economically sensitive, which means that in a weakening economy, default rates could spike.

However, signs of resilient and even accelerating growth in the U.S. economy reduce concerns about growing defaults and non-accruals in the private credit space, meaning that BDC NAVs and lending quality may be stronger than previously thought, while the reduced prospect for aggressive rate cuts from the Fed means that net investment income will likely not be declining as rapidly as previously thought. Despite this positive news, the BDC space has many attractive opportunities in it right now, with several quality BDCs trading at material discounts to their historical averages, including several that actually trade at a discount to their underlying net asset value.

Energy And Commodities Are Sending A Loud Message

This news also bodes well for the energy sector (XLE) as it has historically been a good inflation hedge, and with the economy likely continuing to hum along at a solid clip, that means that energy demand will likely remain strong as well.

Not only that, but the strong performance across the commodities space, especially in gold (GLD), silver (SLV), platinum (PPLT), palladium (PALL), and copper (CPER) recently, means that there will likely be increased upside pressure placed on energy commodities as they're now the clear value play in the commodities space that's otherwise becoming an increasingly crowded trade.

Therefore, I'm more bullish than ever on energy midstream (AMLP) and undervalued high-quality energy producers, as they in particular should shine in an environment where economic growth remains solid and inflation remains at elevated levels, especially when commodities in general are gaining in popularity.

How I’m Positioning My Capital Right Now

The Q3 economic numbers show that net exports and investment made a positive contribution to GDP growth during the quarter and were boosted by recent fiscal and trade policy changes. That being said, the long-term effects of these policy changes remain uncertain, and these initiatives have failed to make a meaningful dent in inflation.

This all amounts to me reducing my expectation of the number of Fed rate cuts moving forward, especially given the apparent ongoing strength of the U.S. economy alongside sticky inflation. This bodes very well for the private credit space, with BDCs and private-credit-heavy alternative asset managers like Blue Owl Capital (OWL) looking very attractively priced right now. Additionally, I like several energy companies in both the midstream space with names like Energy Transfer (ET), moving firmly into an attractive value range, as I recently detailed here, as well as energy production companies like Canadian Natural Resources (CNQ), which I recently detailed here.

As a result, I'm loading up on these sorts of opportunities in my portfolios at High Yield Investor.

data.title: Luckin Coffee: Still Growing, Still Profitable, Still A Buy
data.description:
Eighteen months ago, I recommended buying Luckin Coffee Inc. (LKNCY) when it was trading in the $19 range. In late October 2025 it topped the $40 mark, and since then it has pulled back approximately 15 percent. In my opinion,

  • Recent results have been good,
  • Luckin has invested heavily into its supply chain, and the benefits from this are starting to materialize,
  • The Chinese growth story for Luckin is intact, and,
  • LKNCY’s current trading range represents an attractive entry point for long-term investors.

Accordingly, I re-iterate my Buy recommendation.

I: LKCNY's Corporate Structure

Owners of LKCNY do not own common equity in a Chinese company. Instead, they own an American Depository Security ("ADS"), which in turn owns 8 Class A Shares of a holding company located in the Cayman Islands. The holding company has various agreements with Luckin’s Chinese operating companies under which it receives compensation. Pages 93 and 94 of this regulatory filing have organization charts, and pages F10 onward provide details of Luckin’s various Chinese subsidiaries and the agreements they have entered into with the Cayman Island Holdco.

Of particular interest to me was the discussion regarding Luckin’s historic use of VIEs, or Variable Interest Entities. I first learned of this structure 20 years ago, just before the Credit Crunch.

The VIE structure is now commonly used by Chinese companies that list abroad. Given that the Chinese government frowns upon its use in sensitive industries such as telecoms and technology, I am pleased that Luckin discontinued the use of VIEs several years ago.

Historically, we, through the Beijing WFOE, entered into a series of contractual arrangements with the Former VIE as well as its shareholders so that we could hold certain foreign restricted licenses and permits, such as the internet content provider license (the “ICP license”), through the Former VIE. Though the Former VIE has not been an entity in which we own equity interests or through which we operate business, its financial results had been consolidated into our consolidated financial statements before the Termination Date. See “Item 4. Information on the Company—4.C. Organizational Structure—Former Contractual Arrangements with the Former VIE and Its Nominee Shareholders” for details on these contractual arrangements.

II: How is the demand for coffee in China evolving?

Getting an accurate picture of the market today is difficult due to the amount of conflicting data that have been released. Further, there are a lot of forecasts of huge levels of future growth that don’t appear to be supported by credible assumptions. For example, this article states that coffee consumption in China has been growing by 30 percent a year, but it also presents graphs showing annual growth rates of 0.5% to 4.8% in the three previous years. One report shows the market size at the end of 2024 at $20.9 Billion, while another source reports that the size of the market was $24.6 billion. Others are in the $18 billion range.

So, it's useless (in my opinion) to try and do some type of DCF analysis based on assumed variables such as future market share and profit margins, when there isn't even a consensus on the size of the market today. As for future growth rates? Perhaps there will be a slower rate of growth in China’s coffee market going forward, and perhaps not. Again, if you can't agree on the starting point 10 years ago, or on today's market size, even historic growth rates should be taken with a grain of salt.

Stepping back and taking a bird's eye view, many analysts proclaim that there is a secular movement in China away from tea drinking to coffee drinking. I’m not prepared to make such a claim, and to be honest, I don't think that it's material to the investment case for LKCNY.

Per this article, approximately half of Chinese consumers drink tea daily, while only 27.6 % drink coffee 3 or 4 times a week. To take advantage of this, in Q3 2025, Luckin launched nearly 30 new products, many which were coffee / tea products. It has also aligned its advertising with this revised product mix, through campaigns such “Morning Coffee and Afternoon Tea.” Tea and coffee aren't the only beverages Luckin offers. This quotation comes from Luckin's Q3 '25 Earnings call:

"On the noncoffee side, leveraging our fresh coconut sourcing advantage, we introduced the popular Mango Pomelo Sago, which sold over 12 million cups during the National Day holiday...".

This focus on innovation, convenience, and flavor combinations aligns with trends in the Fast-Moving Consumer Goods sector of China, where in Q1 of 2025,

“Instant coffee saw significant average selling price increases as Chinese consumers gravitated toward high-quality, convenient formats.”

Further confirmation of this trend comes in a December 6, 2025 report from Worldpanel and Bain & Company, which states that,

“In four sub-categories - juice, instant coffee, toothpaste, and sanitary pads - the premium segment outgrew the market overall, driven by innovations and product upgrades.”

Walk into many supermarkets in Chinese Tier 1 and Tier 2 cities, and you’ll see Luckin coffee pods or sachets. Going forward these verticals and channels of distribution should be a source of growth for Luckin. As for secular trends and Chinese consumers' relative preferences for tea versus coffee? In my opinion, a large part of Luckin's Unique Value Proposition will continue to be;

  • Its ability to innovate and launch new products, and,
  • The ease of ordering, paying and arranging delivery of coffee or tea via Luckin's App.

These factors will remain constant across all Luckin stores, and individual outlets will adjust their product mix to suit local demand for tea, coffee, coconut based drinks, or whatever other beverage is in vogue at the time.

III. Luckin has continued to experience profitable growth.

As per Luckin Coffee's 2025 Q3 Investor Presentation dated 17 November 2025, Total Revenue for the quarter was RMB 15.3 billion, or approximately USD 2.14 billion. In local currency terms, this represented a YoY increase of 50.2%, with Company Owned stores showing a YoY increase of 47.7%, and franchisees, or "Partnership Stores" showing YoY Growth of 62.3%.

Operating Income was RMB 1.78 billion, which considering the growth in revenue, is a somewhat disappointing YoY increase of 13%. The comparison to Q3 2023, however, is more favorable - Q3 2025 revenue is 112% greater than it was in Q3 2023, and Operating Income grew by 84%.

Growth often hampers efficiency while new entrants / employees go up the learning curve. Sometimes too, firms cut prices to buy short term growth, margins suffer, and long-term brand positioning and brand loyalty is sacrificed. Luckin doesn’t appear to be making this mistake. Its Gross Margin has held up, and was even slightly improved this quarter to 61%, the highest it's been since Q2 2022. This is despite the fact that Luckin's average number of monthly customer transactions increased to 112.3 million in Q3 '25, up from 91.7 million in Q2 '25, and 79.8 million in Q3 '24. This represents an impressive 23% increase in one quarter, and a 41% increase YoY.

Luckin's improved Gross Margin can be attributed to several factors. First, a price war with Cotti Coffee that lasted for three years is largely over, and some prices have actually risen. While both chains still offer some coffee products at RMB 9.9 (USD 1.40), this appears to be to retain low-income customers and not to take market share.

Second, while the rest of the world struggled with rising coffee bean prices,

"Luckin lowered its supply prices for raw materials, including coffee beans, to its franchisees at the end of last year, with coffee bean prices dropping by a substantial 16.8%."

Luckin is benefiting from past investment in its supply chain. In April of 2024, it opened a state of the art roastery that, with an annual capacity of 30,000 tonnes, is the largest in China. Later, in November of 2024, Luckin announced a $1.38 Billion 5-year 240,000-ton coffee bean procurement agreement with the Brazilian Export and Investment Promotion Agency. This was the second such agreement that Luckin had entered into with Brazil in 2024. It is now largely insulated from rising bean prices, and it appears to be a low cost operator in the China market.

Although Luckin's Gross Margin has improved, the same can't be said for its Operating Margin, which fell to 11.6% from 15.5% in Q3 '24, and 13.4% in Q3 '23.

Luckin is executing quite well on most fronts. The cost of materials has substantially improved compared to previous years (36.2% versus 43.9%), as have both Sales & Marketing Expenses and General Administrative Expenses. However, as can be seen in the graphs above, delivery costs spike in Q3. This is somewhat concerning, and I don't have a firm grasp on the situation. During the most recent investor call, Luckin's CFO attributed this to the delivery platforms they use had subsidizing their services. On the face of it, if a delivery platform charges less, then the cost of deliveries should fall, not rise. Perhaps though, the platforms collect a fixed rate from Luckin and add on a further fee to customers. In such a situation, subsidies could lead to a higher proportion of orders being delivered, thereby leading to the increased costs Luckin experienced.

There isn't clarity on this point, and I don't fully understand it. I note that the CFO also stated that he believes the subsides are being phased out. My assumption is that irrespective of whether these subsidies continue or not, there won’t be any further material deterioration in Luckin's margins, and in the best case, they will rebound.

IV. Future growth will come from Chinese Lower Tier cities.

There are four sources of potential growth for Luckin.

a. New Verticals And Channels of Distribution: One potential source of growth is to increase the number products sold in supermarkets and convenience stores. I do in fact anticipate that over time, as Luckin expands to new cities, it will establish its brand in those markets, and in-fill with products such as pods that are sold in grocery stores. In the short term though, I don't view this as being material enough to move the dial. Luckin is best served using its resources to expand its network across China and pursue infilling opportunities through other channels of distribution at a later time.

b. Same-Store Sales: Another potential source of growth is same-store sales. I had assumed that given its rapid growth, Luckin would already be benefitting from this. My assumptions were that; a) A new store that opened in July 2024 would have experienced operational inefficiencies in its first few first months, and, b) It would take time to build demand, particularly in a 'new coffee' market where neither Luckin's brand or coffee drinking was established. Twelve months later, operational issues would be solved and demand building would have paid fruit. Therefore, one would expect a bump in sales in a YoY comparison. In fact, the opposite appears to have happened. Apparently new outlets were often opened near existing ones, and sales at these locations have been cannibalized. This appears to be a bigger issue for partner stores than in company owned stores.

After experiencing declines in the first three quarters of 2024 (-20.3%, -20.9%, and -13.1% respectively), Luckin managed to narrow the gap significantly in the fourth quarter to just -3.4%, even achieving positive growth in same-store sales for the month of December.

As per Table 1 the situation has improved and the average store now has 480 more transactions (14.2%) than it did a year ago, with most if this growth happening in the last quarter. This has translated into a 14.4% jump in same stores sales at company owned stores in Q3 2025.

Table 1: Average Number of Monthly Transactions, Luckin Coffee*

Q3 2024 Q2 2025 Q3 2025
Average # of Monthly Transactions 71.8 million 91.7 million 112.3 million
Number of Stores at quarter end 21,343 26,206 29,214
Average # of Monthly Transactions per store 3,364 3,499 3,844

*Table prepared by analyst. Data is from Luckin's Q3 '25 Earnings Presentation.

c. International Expansion: Luckin has expanded into three countries in the past few years, Singapore, Malaysia, and the USA. In the most recent quarter, Luckin opened 3,008 new stores, of which 2,979 were in China. I do not expect Luckin to dedicate a large number of resources to international expansion, or for this to be a material source of growth in the next 3 to 5 years for a variety of reasons.

  • On its most recent earnings call, Luckin stated that its expansion into America was exploratory so that it could learn about the American market. This implies that the USA is a long-term project.
  • As well as being one of the richest countries in the world on a per capita basis, Singapore is the home of Luckin's largest investor. It also attracts Chinese entrepreneurs and university students and its digital economy is highly advanced, making it a good fit with Luckin’s online model. Unfortunately, its population is only 6 million, not large enough to move the dial.
  • With a population of 35 million, Malaysia is a bigger country than Singapore. It’s also a coffee producing country with an established coffee culture. However, its $420 Billion GDP would rank it 19th out of 31 Chinese provinces, and Luckin only plans to open 200 stores there in the next three years. Luckin has partnered with a local company, Hextar Industries Berhad, and doubtlessly Luckin will take advantage of similar opportunities in other countries should they arise. The focus though, will be, and should be, China.

d. Continued Expansion In China: Twenty nine thousand stores (18,800 which are company operated) in approximately 300 cities across China is a big footprint. It would be a mistake though, to assume that the market is saturated. China is vast, and there are 23 provinces and a variety of other administrative regions / cities that have powers similar to a province. Most westerners will know of Beijing, Shanghai, Guangzhou, and possibly Chongqing (China's most populous city), Hangzhou, or Shenzhen. Few however will have heard of Ningbo, Jiaxing or Yantai.

The Chinese have adapted an unofficial ranking system, where cities are placed into various tiers based on their level of development and commercial attractiveness. Tier 1 and Tier 2 cities account for approximately 20% of China's population, and a disproportionate amount of GDP.

Luckin originally concentrated on Tier 1 cities like Beijing, Shanghai, Guanzhou after which it expanded into "New Tier 1" and Tier 2 cities such as Hangzhou, Suzhou and Wuhan. These cities are large (i.e., 10 million plus), prosperous, coastal, and often a large proportion of the work force is white collar. Anecdotal reports are that approximately 50 percent of Luckin's stores are in these types of cities, and while there is some growth, its strategy in these core markets has to do more with optimization and rationalization of its network.

In recent years, Luckin has started to enter Tier 3 and lower cities. Collectively,

  • Per the map above, they account for 80 percent of China's population,
  • They comprise more than 60 percent of China's GDP, and,
  • They are growing faster than larger cities.

There are a variety of reasons that are cited for their higher rate of growth. This include cheaper land for manufacturing plants, cheaper housing, construction workers returning to their home provinces due to China's property market bust, and national and local government policies designed to accelerate growth in lower tier cities. According to a July 2024 report produced by McKinsey,

... one of the most striking findings from our survey is the substantial difference in confidence levels between millennials (ages 26-41) in higher-tier cities and lower-tier cities. Of the millennial consumers we surveyed in lower-tier cities, 75 percent expressed confidence in China’s economy, compared with 65 percent of millennials in higher-tier cities. This confidence gap widens even further when we look at mid- and high-income millennials in tier-3 and tier-4 cities, where confidence levels exceed 80 percent.

The report attributes this higher degree of optimism to the facts that millennials living in lower tier cities have better job security and more disposable income than their counterparts in larger cities. As a result, a geographic split in the retail sales has emerged. According to Bain & Company, in the first 9 months of 2024,

"...Tier 3 and Tier 4 cities outperformed Tier 1 and Tier 2 cities (see Figure 5). For Q3 year-to-date, Tier 3 and Tier 4 cities grew by +2.9% and +5.5%, respectively, while Tier 1 and Tier 2 cities faced declines of -4.4% and -0.9%."

So there is plenty of runway in China for Luckin to continue its expansion.

V. Luckin's Valuation Looks Reasonable, Especially Considering Its Growth Trajectory

One of the reasons that I like Luckin is that despite the fact that it is growing at a double-digit rate, there appears to be little danger of dilution. Its expansion is being funded through franchising, and from cashflow generated by the company itself. In 2024 Luckin produced positive operating cash flow of approximately $1 billion, and that is roughly what they invested in new company stores. The same pattern appears to be repeating in 2025.

In order to form an opinion regarding LKCNY’s valuation, I looked at three comps. Starbucks (SBUX), which I note recently sold its 60 percent of China operations, is the first. Mixue (MXUBY), with 40,000 fast food locations in China is the second, and finally, I compared Luckin to Yum China (YUMC), the owner and operator of a number of fast food and beverage chains in China.

Table 2: Select Financial Information LKCNY & Competitors

Luckin Starbucks Mixue Group Yum China
Market Cap $10.98 Bil $96.18 Bil 20.59 Bil $17.40 Bil
P/E GAAP (FWD) 20.58 35.55 __ 19.83
P/E GAAP (TTM) 20.17 51.88 27.14 20.77
PEG GAAP (TTM) 0.34 NM 0.65 3.12
Revenue Growth YoY 44.47% 2.79% 22.29% 3.29%
EBITDA Growth (TTM) 83.20% -16.87% 42.82% 8.78%
Gross Profit Margin 61.97% 23.06% 32.26% 20.38%
Net Income Margin 8.47% 4.99% 18.07% 7.81%
Total Debt / Equity 41.48% NM 0.52% 28.83%
Current Ratio 1.66 0.72 3.23 1.32

Luckin Coffee is the fastest growing of all four companies by a long shot, yet its valuation as measured by both P/E and PEG Ratios is the lowest of the group. Luckins Margins, especially its Gross Margin, are superior to all, and only Mixue has a better Net Income Margin. Debt is manageable, and there is ample liquidity. In my opinion, LKCNY represents good value at these levels.

Conclusion

When I first looked at Luckin Coffee, it had just emerged from the shadow of well-known corporate issues, and it was in the early stage of a price war with Cotti Coffee. However, it was growing at 70 percent per year while remaining profitable and cash flow positive. I thought it was a good value, and it was.

Now, eighteen months later, the price war is over, and the company is more mature. It's built China's largest roastery and pursued a vertical integration strategy, while locking in a 5-year supply of coffee. The store count has more than tripled, and it has entered three new countries. It has also established a track record of innovation, and its products have started to appear on supermarket shelves.

The company is still growing rapidly while remaining profitable. Its ROE was 25%, today its 27%. Financial leverage has decreased, and its margins are even better than they were at the time, i.e., the Gross Margin is 62% versus 55% in June 2024.

As for its valuation? It’s very similar - the P/E Ratio was 18.5 and now its 20. The PEG was 0.16, and now its 0.34. Once again, I think that Luckin Coffee is good value. Accordingly, I reiterate my Buy recommendation.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: 2025: The 'Market Rotation' That Really Wasn't, Tech Dominated - Ditto In 2026
data.description:
Today's article will be short and sweet. Reading all the financial analysts in print this year, and listening to them on leading financial networks like CNBC and Bloomberg, an ordinary investor like myself would likely have gotten the strong impression that 2025 was the year the market 'broadened out' and brought the technology sector back down to Earth. Dividend 'value' stocks were supposed to have outperformed while the valuation of technology stocks would come crashing down because AI was nothing but 'hype'. The equal weight S&P500 was the place to be, they said, because the S&P500 was 'massively overweight' in technology companies that were 'significantly overvalued'. Yet I would argue that that narrative never really happened (see chart below). And my message to my followers and all investors today is: don't believe that narrative for 2026 either: technology companies will continue to lead the market again next year.

As you can see from the chart above, the top-two sector performers were, once again, the Technology Sector SPDR ETF (XLK) and the Communication Services Sector SPDR (XLC). While I concede the Financial & Industrials sectors, (XLF) and (XLI), respectively, closed the performance gap some, the highly promoted Health-Care (XLV) and Energy (XLE) sectors once again significantly lagged the broad market averages by a wide margin.

In addition, it is notable that the tech sector still outperformed despite a significant sell-off in December.

Equal Weight & "Value"?

Ok, so what about the S&P equal-weight and "value" themes?

Nope. As you can see from the chart above, the Invesco S&P500 Equal-Weight ETF (RSP) not only significantly under-performed the XLK technology ETF, it even lagged the plain old S&P500 ETF (VOO) by a whopping 5.6%. Value stocks did a bit better than the equal-weight RSP thesis, but still significantly lagged both the technology sector and the S&P500.

Dividend Stocks?

Well, how about dividend stocks? They were supposed to significantly outperform the "over-valued" technology stocks this year, right? So let's see how they did:

Wrong again. As you can see from the graphic, the total returns of some of the leading dividend centric ETFs, like (SCHD), (VIG), and (VYM) significantly under-performed the plain-old S&P500 let alone the technology sector XLK ETF.

I hate to keep picking on the SCHD ETF, but it continues to amaze me how - seemingly almost every day, day-after-day - the Seeking Alpha homepage 'leaderboard' has a bullish article on SCHD posted at the very top. Years ago this observation motivated me to write one of my all-time most read articles on Seeking Alpha: "Retirees Beware: Dividend Investing Is Overrated". Despite the many naysayers that argued with that article in the comments (published 5-years ago), I am proud of the advice I gave to investors in that piece and, at this point, feel my perspective has been fully vindicated with the passage of time.

Gold & Silver

What certainly did 'broaden out' this year was the price of gold and silver!

As you can see from that graphic, and despite gold seemingly sucking all the oxygen out of the room, silver's performance this year was ~2x that of gold. Both clearly and significantly outperformed both the S&P500 and the technology sector.

At this point, I think the silver rally is way overblown and expect a deep pullback sooner rather than later. Yes, silver has much more industrial demand as compared to gold, but it is also much more abundant in the Earth's crust.

Meantime, I continue to be bullish on gold for a number of reasons:

  1. Concerns about the independence of the U.S. Federal Reserve and the recent lowering of the Fed's discount rate despite inflation being closer to 3% than to the Fed target of 2%. This has likely raised concerns not just in the eyes of domestic but - perhaps more importantly - the global investors who finance America's debt as well.
  2. The loss of American Exceptionalism (see "Gone in 90-Days") due to President Trump's arguably unwise and chaotic tariff, trade, economic, governmental (think DOGE), social, and foreign policies. This has arguably led to America's traditional Democratic & NATO allies to no longer trust the United States when it comes to the two issues they care most about: trade and national security.
  3. The combination of 1. & 2. above have led to what I consider to believe is and will continue to be a secular long-term bear-market in the value of the U.S. dollar (see graphic below).
  4. Central bank buying of gold is likely to continue given a loss-of-faith in the value of the U.S. dollar, combined with the massive geopolitical uncertainty of the Trump administration's economic policies and foreign policies - including recent military adventurism in Venezuela and rhetoric by Trump that he "has to have" Greenland, an autonomous territory in the Kingdom of Denmark (a founding member of NATO).

As you can see, while gold soared this year, the value of (our) U.S. dollar sank a massive 9.7%.

In my opinion, gold was one of the easiest calls ever this year. Back in April, in my Seeking Alpha piece "The 4-Fund Trump Portfolio" (an Editor's Pick), I recommended the Van Eck Junior Gold Miners ETF (GDXJ), which has been an absolute monster this year, blowing away the S&P500 and technology sector:

That suggestion was notable considering that I have long advised investors to stick with U.S. minted gold coins over "paper gold" miners, which for years I watched fritter away shareholder capital on acquisitions (i.e. Newmont, Barrick, etc) and underperform the price of gold itself. However, this year I was so bullish on gold that even I myself bought some GDXJ, as I expected the leverage of the miners to outperform. I should also note that despite the name, the GDXJ ETF also has excellent exposure to silver. That being the case, shareholders like me should be on alert for the silver price correction I see coming next year.

Tech Stocks - Stay The Course

Meantime, the leading technology companies did great again this year, with all three of my top picks - Google (GOOG), Broadcom (AVGO), and the VanEck Semiconductor ETF (SMH) - significantly outperforming both the broad technology sector and S&P500:

And I continue to be bullish on the broad technology sector (and these same three companies) heading into 2026 for all the reasons I recently gave in my Seeking Alpha article "Tech ETF Faceoff: FTEC Vs. XLK":

"In addition, all the leading technology companies generally have some very attractive investment attributes in common:

  • They all have high-quality and leading global brands.
  • They have built platforms and/or businesses that have wide moats.
  • They have very strong free-cash-flow profiles.
  • They have excellent growth potential moving forward.
  • They generate a significant percentage of revenue overseas, providing currency risk mitigation and protection from a falling U.S. dollar.
  • Many operate software-as-a-service (SaaS) based platforms that can be very easily and economically scaled up as new customers are added."

And, of course, the AI data center theme is a primary catalyst for the tech sector, and I fully expect the boom to continue and even accelerate in 2026. It is important to note that none of the 'Big 3' hyperscalers - Amazon (AMZN), Microsoft (MSFT), and Google (GOOG) - or other leading tech companies like NVIDIA and Broadcom - need to borrow money to build-out AI data-center and infrastructure. All of them are generating very strong free-cash-flow despite investing in the AI-boom. That is because demand for their products and services remain very strong - revenue is growing and so are profits.

See a recent Seeking Alpha post: "NVidia, Broadcom 'ripe for outperformance' in 2026". I agree 100%.

Summary & Conclusions

Once again this year, and despite the popular narrative being spun by financial analysts to reduce exposure to the technology sector, I believe my long-term advice for investors to build a well-diversified portfolio on the foundation of a high-quality low-cost S&P500 ETF (like VOO, for instance) and to over-weight the technology sector has been vindicated by the market's performance.

Meantime, my advice that such a well-diversified portfolio own some gold & silver has also proven to be spot-on (pun intended...). In fact, I think that the higher net-worth an investor has, the larger percentage allocation to gold & silver he or she should have because, after all, that investor has much more to lose in the event of continued weakness in the U.S. dollar, higher inflation, or a complete financial meltdown due to what I consider to be very unwise policies by the current Trump administration.

Bottom line? Stick with the plan: build and hold a well-diversified portfolio (on the foundation of the S&P500 and over-weight the technology sector) through the market's up-and-down cycles, adding to high-quality investments during sell-offs like this one: "Broadcom: What Many Analysts And Investors Got Wrong - Buy The Dip".

With all that said, let me take this opportunity to wish you and yours a very Happy, Healthy, and - of course - a most prosperous Holiday Season and New Year! And thanks for reading my stuff this year, I appreciate it!

data.title: STMicroelectronics: Likely Undervalued, But Recovery Will Take Time
data.description:
Many semiconductor companies with heavy exposure to auto and industrial end-markets have underperformed since late 2023, but STMicroelectronics (STM) (“STMicro”) has been hit especially hard by specific customer exposures, share loss in its microcontroller business, and price pressure across its lines, with weaker volumes exacerbating the problem through lower fab utilization.

Down about 45% since my last update, STMicro has meaningfully underperformed peers, rivals, and comps like onsemi (ON), Microchip (MCHP), Infineon (IFNNY), Texas Instruments (TXN), and NXP Semiconductors (NXPI), largely due to those aforementioned challenges, as well as a lack of differentiated near-term growth drivers.

STMicro will recover in time, but I do have concerns that it could take multiple quarters before the shares attract much positive attention from analysts or institutional investors, as more meaningful recoveries in auto and industrial markets may well be a 2H’26 phenomenon. The shares do look undervalued and margins may snap back faster than I expect, but it will take some patience for this to work out.

Better Is Not Necessarily “Good” – End-Market Recoveries May Take A Bit Longer

STMicro should see a small sequential uptick in revenue when it reports fourth quarter results in January 2026, and that will be the third quarter in a row if it does, but business will still be lacking in momentum, and revenue should still be down year over year.

As things sit now, inventories in auto and industrial end-markets (roughly 60% of the revenue base), have been improving, but still appear to be above likely new normalized levels (I say “likely” as normal inventory levels have shifted post-pandemic and don’t seem likely to return to prior long-term norms). Underlying auto production volumes are not particularly strong outside of China, and while multiple industrial companies have spoken of improving short-cycle activity, this is modest improvement (low to mid-single-digit growth) off a lower base.

Looking at 2026, it’s hard to feel strongly bullish about these two critical markets. S&P is looking for a slight year-over-year decline in auto production volumes in 2026, and looking at recent comments from auto parts suppliers and some chip companies (including Infineon), that outlook seems to be regarded as a bit bullish or optimistic. Moreover, EV volumes seem likely to slow in China, and Tesla (TSLA), a long-standing major customer of STMicro, is struggling to regain momentum.

On a more positive note, I do see opportunities for semi content growth to offset weaker underlying volumes. ADAS, infotainment, and interior/systems electrification should drive better volumes for categories like MCUs and sensors (and some power components to a lesser extent), and STMicro has leverage there. Still, the ongoing shifts away from electrification in Western markets is a significant challenge for STMicro’s SiC power business.

Turning to the industrial end-market, I’m more bullish on improving demand for industrial electrical equipment and automation, but that’s going to take time to develop and is likely to be more of a second-half phenomenon, with orders coming in ahead of those improved revenues. I do expect increasing interest in SiC and GaN power components in industrial electrical components, but other opportunities like advanced robotics and edge AI/machine learning will take time to develop.

Idiosyncratic Drivers Have Largely Turned Negative

What has disappointed me most about STMicro relative to my last article is how a lot of differentiation here has turned negative. I don’t fault the company for downturns in auto and industrial markets, and likewise there isn’t a lot the company can do about increasing competition from Chinese microcontroller and power semiconductor players, but I feel like the company has come up short elsewhere.

Tesla has long been the major customer for STMicro’s EV power semiconductor business, and the company has certainly seen outsized pressure on its business from Tesla’s much-publicized challenges, but the company hasn’t done as well as I expected in gaining business with other OEMs. Now, with many Western OEMs backing off prior EV plans and Chinese OEMs increasingly looking to source from Chinese companies, it’s looking like volume leverage in this business is going to take longer to develop.

I’m also concerned about recent share losses in the MCU space. As per Gartner’s 2024 report, STMicro appears to have lost almost four points of share, pushing it down to fourth place behind Infineon, NXP, and Renesas (and ahead of Microchip, who has lost even more share). While the share losses haven’t been as bad in auto MCUs, STMicro lost more than seven points of share in industrial and consumer MCU markets.

Management has talked about this in the context of inventory corrections, but I think there may be more to it than that. Competition from lower-priced Chinese MCUs has certainly played some role, but I’m more concerned about the significant (roughly three-point) share gains at Infineon and NXP, as they seem to be winning business that used to go toward STMicro. What’s more, with Renesas (RNECY) turning around its own MCU business, I’m concerned that the 2025 share numbers may look even worse for STMicro for what was once one of the wider-moat businesses.

Development of the power business has likewise been disappointing. On one hand, I don’t blame STMicro for the industry-wide glut in silicon carbide nor the slower than expected uptake of GaN-based power devices, but I feel that Infineon has done a better job of positioning itself for opportunities like data center power (particularly the transition to 800V power), and it is my opinion that STMicro has come up short of its own prior targets for leveraging its core power technologies into new markets/applications.

The Outlook

It’s not all bad news for STMicro. The company has gained content with Apple (AAPL), and that has helped drive some growth in the consumer electronics space. I also expect this to be an area of relative strength around midyear (Q2 and Q3) ahead of broader end-market recoveries.

I also like the company’s efforts to refine and reposition its manufacturing base; while the company is taking a significant hit now from underutilized capacity (capacity utilization was below 70% in Q3’25), there are growing reports that ongoing growth in data center/AI-related chip production may lead to capacity shortfalls if, when, and as auto and industrial demand recover.

As far as longer-term growth drivers go, I’m still a believer in STMicro’s business; in the near-term that will be driven more by sensors, MCUs, and ancillary power (for ADAS, infotainment/interior, and so on), but I still see long-term EV power opportunities. I’m likewise still a firm believer in industrial automation and electrification, which will drive demand for more advanced power chips, MCUs, and sensors, particularly in more advanced robotics.

Looking at data center and AI opportunities, management is looking for around $500M in incremental revenue by 2030, with management specifically pointing to silicon photonics. I’d prefer to see more focus on gaining share in areas like vertical power delivery and power sidecars, but time will tell. Management has also talked about $600M in incremental opportunities from edge AI (largely on the MCU side); STMicro’s strong historic presence and engineering capabilities will help (including new products like the 18nm STM32V8), but this is a space that will see ample competition (with companies like Synaptics (SYNA) recently launching specialized product lines for this application).

Turning to the financials, I’m looking for high-single-digit revenue growth in 2026 (around 7%); I’m a bit below the average sell-estimate now mostly just because of caution on the auto and industrial markets. I expect revenue to accelerate to double-digit growth for a couple of years thereafter, driving five-year growth of around 5% and longer-term growth close to 6% on the back of auto electrification and digitalization, as well as increased electrification and automation of industrial markets.

On the margin side I expect improved utilization to drive gross margin back to the mid-30%’s in FY’26 (from 33.2% in Q3’25), but a return to 40%+ gross margin could take three years. Likewise, mid-teens operating margin seems unlikely before 2028. Longer term, I expect low double-digit free cash flow margins (driving around 5% of long-term adjusted FCF growth). I still believe that mid-teens FCF margins are possible (as per my last article), but I need more confidence that the company can win and hold share in higher-value power and MCU markets.

Between discounted cash flow and margin-driven EV/revenue (there is a long-standing relationship between semi company margins and multiples) I believe fair value is in the $28’s today. Roughly speaking every point of operating margin in FY’28 is worth about $2/share in incremental value, with my current base case using a 2.45x revenue multiple and including anticipated funding needs for near-term capacity expansion.

The Bottom Line

I think the biggest positive argument for the shares is a faster/stronger-than-expected recovery that unlocks more sales growth and margin leverage than I model. The biggest negative may be more debatable; for me, it’s that management has missed prior targets and struggled to really set the company apart as a share-gaining semi company with positive idiosyncratic drivers and distinct moats. In my view, the company has been more “commodity-adjacent” than I’d like during this recent turndown.

STMicro does look undervalued today, and there could be more operating leverage than I expect if the recovery accelerates. The “but” is that I think STMicro’s reported financials could be pretty lackluster until the second half of 2026, and the shares could drift in the meantime. It’s worth a spot on a watchlist, but I’d like more evidence of positive momentum before buying even if that costs me some upside.
